entries.title: Matas Buzelis talks about Billy Donovan as Bulls officially eliminated
entries.summary: <p>OKLAHOMA CITY – Matas Buzelis doesn’t know how the story will end.</p><p>Not his or that of Bulls coach Billy Donovan.</p><p>What the second year forward could present as his truth is he would not be the player he was – or on his way to becoming – if it wasn’t for Donovan’s guidance. So the idea that the Sun-Times reported earlier this week that there was growing momentum around the coach possibly wrapping up his stay with the Bulls, well, not the type of news Buzelis wanted to embrace.</p><p>“He’s been everything, man,” Buzelis said of Donovan. “He’s been a role model to me, he’s someone I look up to as a person, as a human being. He’s a guy that’s been a cornerstone for me. He’s pushed me to my limits and he’s going to keep pushing me until I can’t be pushed anymore. I can’t thank somebody like that enough. He tells you the truth every time. It’s hard to do that, especially in this league, to have someone in your corner that is going to tell you everything you need to hear.”</p><p>Donovan addressed the report on Wednesday, and all he would say, and more than once, was he was looking forward to sitting down with both ownership and the front office at the end of the season to get a grasp on the plan moving forward to turn this around and start winning.</p><p>As far as Buzelis was concerned, Donovan was by far the best qualified to coach that turnaround, especially as far as how Buzelis prefers to be coached. From Day 1 as a rookie, Donovan has handed him nothing. Minutes have been earned, not gifted, and the hook has been a quick one at times.</p><p>“This might sound like crazy but it’s the truth, he’s teaching me how to be a winning player, and I know everyone will say, ‘Well, you’re not winning a lot of games,’ but he’s taught me the characteristics it takes to win and that’s all you can ask for,” Buzelis said. “Even when he’s sat me down after a few minutes (into a game), that’s just about telling me what I have to do, what I need to do to be better at it, and a guy like me I know my time will come.”</p><p>Just not against the defending NBA Champions on Friday. While the Bulls (29-44) put up a fight for almost three quarters, the talent deficit flexed its muscles going into the final stanza. And with the 131-113 loss, Donovan’s crew was officially eliminated from even reaching play-in status this season.</p><p>What Donovan liked against the Thunder – and hopes to see moving forward over the final weeks – is a roster that doesn’t quit until the horn of the final game sounds.</p><p>“It comes down to how professional you are, and how important these games are,” Donovan said. “How do you internally look at that. I do think a lot of that speaks to character quite honestly. I think we have a bunch of good guys in that locker room, and I would bet on them that they would go out there and put their best foot forward, try and do the best they can. The competitive character reveals itself.”</p><p>It did for Oklahoma City as they overcame a nine-point deficit and pulled away in the fourth by starting off that final quarter with a 19-6 run.</p><p>The Bulls were led by Collin Sexton’s 22 points, while Buzelis finished with 15.</p><p>“I’m going to keep grinding, keep trying to push everybody,” Buzelis said of the final nine remaining games. “We’re not just going to bull(bleep) these next few games we have left. We’re going to try and learn, I’m going to continue to try and learn, and that’s it.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Blackhawks' ultra-young core faces gut check after back-to-back blowout losses
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — The idea of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> dressing one of the youngest lineups in modern NHL history is romantic. The reality, at least for now, is less pleasing on the eyes.</p><p>The Hawks were shellacked 6-1 by a poor Rangers team Friday, one day after getting <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/26/blackhawks-flyers-poor-defense-louis-crevier-sacha-boisvert" target="_blank" >routed 5-1</a> by a mediocre Flyers team.</p><p>It was almost as ugly a back-to-back set as the Hawks’ disastrous December weekend in Southern California, when the Kings and Ducks beat them by a combined 13-1.</p><p>General manager Kyle Davidson, who has accompanied the team on this East Coast trip, surely hopes the fully developed version of this lineup will fare much better than this fresh-off-the-vine version.</p><p>And it likely will, to be fair. But the results this week offer a bitter reminder the Hawks still have a long way to go to become contenders, even if they’ve progressed through one stage of the rebuild and into the next.</p><p>With all six defensemen in the lineup 24 or younger and seven of 12 forwards 23 or younger, the Hawks played a solid first period, then fell apart.</p><p>‘‘We were in the game,’’ said new alternate captain Tyler Bertuzzi, one of few remaining veterans. ‘‘And then I don’t know what happened in the second and third.</p><p>‘‘We’re trying to figure that out ourselves. I don’t know if it’s just such a young team [with] immaturity and we can’t put it together, but it’s on everyone. It’s on us older guys to show the lead and have them follow. As a group — everyone — the last few games, we have not been good at all.’’</p><p>Through the first three games of this trip, scoring chances during five-on-five play favor Hawks opponents 130-59 and high-danger chances favor opponents 69-23. Those are lopsided numbers.</p><p>The Hawks’ biggest problem Thursday was ill-advised step-ups in the neutral zone leading to odd-man rushes against them. Their biggest problem Friday was succumbing to pressure in the defensive zone and failing to relieve it cleanly.</p><p>Fatigue is almost certainly a factor, considering the density of games and lack of practice time lately. But the schedule is dense for every team, so it’s no excuse.</p><p>‘‘This will be a good test for us here after two tough ones,’’ Blashill said. ‘‘Overall, we haven’t been in this spot a lot. I think we’ve played pretty good hockey through most of that stretch after the trade deadline. Now we’ve got to regroup.’’</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Rinzel couldn't clear and Robertson finished the play: <a href="https://t.co/GW9ytdwux7">pic.twitter.com/GW9ytdwux7</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2037684563787313196?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 28, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The Rangers, who had lost six in a row entering the game, moved into a tie with the Hawks for 30th place in the standings — a close race for the second-best odds in the draft lottery. (The Flames, one point ahead of both, also remain in that conversation.)</p><p>For that reason, a sizable contingent of Rangers fans on social media were rooting for their team to lose and a smaller contingent of extremely future-looking Hawks fans probably were pleased with the result.</p><p></p><p>But most Hawks fans have grown tired of caring about lottery position, even if they recognize another top-five pick will be valuable. The Hawks’ front office has shifted its primary focus to the NHL roster and its progress, too. After all, the purpose of this season is to take the first step up the mountain, not to tank.</p><p></p><p>Even though the Hawks remain on pace to finish with 75 points — a 14-point improvement from last season — this week is a reasonable cause for concern.</p><p></p><p>‘‘We’ve got a lot of the . . . core pieces that that are going to be here moving forward, hopefully,’’ defenseman Alex Vlasic said. ‘‘So it’s on us to do a better job.’’</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Activists protest ICE agents at O'Hare Airport
entries.summary: <p>Demonstrators gathered Friday evening at O’Hare International Airport to protest U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents who have been called to assist with airport security amid a partial federal government shutdown.</p><p>ICE agents <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/23/ice-agents-ohare-airport-partial-government-shutdown" target="_blank" >had been seen</a> at O’Hare since Monday after President Donald Trump directed them to help the Transportation Security Administration’s operations at airports nationwide amid the Department of Homeland Security funding lapse.</p><p>No ICE agents were seen Friday in Terminal 1, where the couple dozen protesters gathered, or Terminal 2.</p><p>“ICE out of O’Hare, ICE out of everywhere,” protesters shouted.</p><p>After an hour of standing outside at the departures area, the protesters walked inside Terminal 1, chanting, “ICE out now.” Many travelers and airline workers laughed and took videos of the protesters. One man pointed in protesters’ faces and shouted, “Get out now.”</p><p>Once demonstrators returned to their initial staging area outside, Chicago police officers issued a dispersal order for unlawful assembly, which was triggered when the protesters went inside. Police vehicles then blocked access to the Terminal 1 drop off area. No arrests were reported.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d20000" class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Earlier Friday, Trump signed an executive action that will pay TSA workers, who have gone without pay for weeks since the DHS shutdown began Feb. 14.</p><p>The TSA has faced staffing shortages during the shutdown as Democrats and Republicans in Congress quarrel over funding for DHS, which oversees the TSA, ICE and the U.S. Customs and Border Protection. Democrats have refused to fund DHS without changes to immigration enforcement practices.</p><p>The shortage has resulted in long lines for security checkpoints at airports across the country. Travelers at O’Hare have experienced lengthy lines during busy travel weekends, however, many travelers have recently reported wait times of less than half an hour.</p><p>About 75 ICE agents were expected to be stationed at O’Hare, Mayor Brandon Johnson said earlier this week. Agents were monitoring exit lanes and checkpoints, not performing security screenings. Agents were not deployed to Midway.</p><p>Friday’s protesters pointed to the Trump administration’s aggressive deportation campaign last fall in the Chicago area, “Operation Midway Blitz,” that brought an influx of ICE and CBP agents who roved the streets and made thousands of arrests. The operation involved a seemingly indiscriminate use of tear gas and other chemical irritants, and the fatal shooting of Silverio Villegas Gonzalez and the shooting of Marimar Martinez at the hands of federal agents.</p><p>“I don’t like what’s going on in our country with ICE agents terrorizing our communities,” said Melissa Espinoza, 45, who calmly stood along protest-designated fencing holding a sign reading, “ICE out now.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e80000" entries.title: Evanston's Justin Johnson on fast track to Illinois
entries.summary: <p>Evanston junior defensive back Justin Johnson is a relative newcomer to football, but he figured out a way to get on Power Five programs’ radar.</p><p>He ran a personal-best 10.64 seconds in the 100 meters to win a medal at the IHSA Class 3A state track and field finals last spring. Johnson is a consensus four-star prospect, according to 247Sports.com, and the state’s No. 12 player in the junior class.</p><p>His game-breaking speed — he’s also a returning medalist in the 200 — helped him land seven Big Ten offers, and he recently became Illinois’ first local commit in the 2027 recruiting cycle.</p><p>“They’ve been recruiting me since last fall,” said Johnson, who has been running track for years but didn’t start playing football till high school.</p><p>The 6-2, 180-pounder has plenty of ties to the school — both of his parents went there, and Evanston coach Miles Osei played for the Fighting Illini. But Johnson said no one pushed him toward coach Bret Bielema’s program.</p><p>“Coach Osei, he didn’t want to tell me to go anywhere,” Johnson said.</p><p>And he said his parents just wanted him to find a place that felt right. It wound up being Illinois.</p><p>The recruiting process could be enjoyable and nerve-racking, he said.</p><p>“I feel it was definitely fun to go to different schools and experience the different vibes going on,” Johnson said, “It was [also] stressful . . . trying to be my best version [of myself] talking to coaches.”</p><p>Johnson has one more season each of track and football, as he plans to graduate early and join the Illini for spring practice in 2027.</p><p>“I’m taking this year [of track] super serious,” he said. “I’m trying to win at least one gold.”</p><p>Then it will be on to his final prep football season. The Wildkits were 3-6 in Osei’s first season and aim to get back to the state playoffs for the first time since 2021. The commitment to improve is there, Johnson said.</p><p>“This offseason has been a grind, 6:30 lifts every morning,” he said.</p><p><b>Carmel gains QB</b></p><p>Reed Dangel, Warren’s starting quarterback last season, announced on social media that he’s transferring to Carmel for his senior year.</p><p>The Corsairs had an opening at the position when Nebraska commit Trae Taylor left after his junior season, transferring to Millard South in Omaha, Nebraska. Taylor is a top-five player nationally at the position who accounted for 4,204 total yards and 50 touchdowns last season.</p><p>Dangel had 624 total yards and two TD passes in 2025 in an offense tilted toward the running of Illinois wrestling recruit Aaron Stewart, who had 2,872 yards and 46 TDs.</p><p><b>Switching classes</b></p><p>Two notable seniors from last season announced on social media that they’re heading to prep school and reclassifying to the 2027 class.</p><p>Simeon wide receiver Raymond Liggins III is going to The Peddie School in New Jersey, and Nazareth quarterback Jackson Failla is going to Phillips Exeter Academy in New Hampshire. Liggins has offers from Miami, Kentucky and Missouri, among others, in the 2027 recruiting cycle.<br></p>
entries.author: Mike Clark

entries.title: Bulls coach Billy Donovan discusses the NBA's anti-tanking proposals
entries.summary: <p>OKLAHOMA CITY — Billy Donovan didn’t even get close to reading the fine print. There will be plenty of time for the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls</a> coach after the season ends in a few weeks</p><p>What Donovan did know was the NBA presented three different anti-tanking concepts to the board of governors that will now be discussed and broken down, and that the league was looking to add more teams into the lottery process moving forward.</p><p>The details of those three scenarios and the changes that will come to the team building landscape? That was above Donovan’s pay grade.</p><p>“I saw more teams possibly in the lottery and they are trying to get teams to compete to the best of their ability, but people are always going to look at reason to do for what’s best for their organization and how they need to go about doing it,” Donovan said on Friday. “It could also be a situation too, and I’m sure they are going to take it into consideration, if you do have a team that’s maybe very young, a lot of first-year players, they’re trying to build out of it and maybe the team’s not good enough right now, you could get caught several years even though you’re trying to put your best foot forward where you don’t get the picks necessary, so I get the incentive part but there may be some talk on what about the teams that maybe really are at the bottom, how do they move out if year after year they’re not in position through assets or accumulation of draft picks to really get out of that place? I don’t know what the right answer or solution is.”</p><p>As of now no one really does.</p><p>According to reports, the first proposal would be 18 teams in the lottery, flattened odds with the bottom 10 teams having an 8% chance of winning, while the remaining 20% of the odds distributed in decreasing order for 11 through 18 in a lottery drawing for all 18 picks.</p><p>The second proposal involves 22 teams in the lottery using a two-year record. It would involve lottery teams reaching a minimum win total floor in each season. Teams that fall short then get slotted to meet that floor.</p><p>The final proposal is again 18 teams in a five-by-five lottery where the bottom five teams have equal odds of hitting No. 1, a lottery formed from those top five, and the bottom five teams have a floor at 10. Those that fall out of the top five would then get sorted in a separate drawing.</p><p>Whichever way the league goes won’t affect the Bulls in this season’s lottery, where they will be hoping for luck to smile upon them.</p><p></p><h3><b>Out of time</b></h3><p></p><p>Donovan said it was an easy decision for the medical staff to shut down both Jaden Ivey (knee) and Jalen Smith (calf) for the rest of the season, simply because there was not enough time to get them rehabbed and back on the floor.</p><p>“With Jalen there was probably going to have to be another week to 10 days of rehab and then a ramp up, and then you’re moving to the end of the season,” Donovan said. “Same thing was felt with Jaden as well, where he’s made some progress and gains in strength, but he’s not all the way there.”</p><p></p><h3><b>Slim pickings</b></h3><p></p><p>With Jalen Smith out and Nick Richards banged up from the Philadelphia loss with a bad elbow, Donovan’s starting five against Oklahoma City was again on the small end in the frontcourt, going with Matas Buzelis, Isaac Okoro and Guerschon Yabusele up front and Tre Jones and Josh Giddey in the backcourt.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore entries.title: Upstart Norwegian club Bodø/Glimt goes toe-to-toe with European titans in Champions League
entries.summary: <p>LAS VEGAS — Sporting Lisbon turned up the Champions League heat on the Arctic Minnows.</p><p>The Norwegian club Bodø/Glimt, from a small fishing village on the chilly side of the Arctic Circle, could only last so long, so far out over its skis.</p><p>Excellent CBS Sports play-by-play announcer Chris Wittyngham called it something like “Buddha,” lexicon we adopt gladly.</p><p>“Counter-punchers supreme,” he called Buddha, which beat Manchester City, Atletico Madrid and Serie A leader Inter Milan twice in the run-up to its round-of-16, two-leg series against Sporting.</p><p>When the competition started in late August, Buddha’s CL title odds were 500-to-1 at the Westgate SuperBook, 1,000-1 at Circa Sports.</p><p>Sweet profits could have been extracted along the way. For instance, Buddha was +830 to defeat Inter in their second leg; the Norwegians won 2-1 before 70,441 at the hallowed San Siro in Milan.</p><p>When it beat Sporting 3-0 before 7,971 inside its Aspmyra Stadion on March 11, Buddha’s title odds were 150-1.</p><p>Its balloon popped in the second leg, when Sporting popped in four goals in the final 30 minutes of regulation and 30 of extra time. Sporting won 5-3.</p><p>Sporting attacker Pedro “Pote” Gonçalves, who missed that first match, made a magnificent difference in the second.</p><p>Buddha parked the bus to focus on defense, which backfired spectacularly. Sporting had huge advantages in corners (16-4) and shots (38-9), advancing to play Arsenal.</p><p>Bye, bye, Arctic Minnows.</p><p>“We did not play the game, we played the occasion,” Buddha gaffer Kjetil Knutsen told the BBC, “and it became far too big for us.”</p><h3>No rest</h3><p>Against Euro titans FC Barcelona, Real Madrid, Bayern Munich, Borussia Dortmund, AC Milan and Inter Milan and defending champ Paris Saint-Germain (PSG), squads like Buddha get squished, typically early.</p><p>Buddha, though, became the northernmost team to reach such a late CL stage.</p><p>Plus, it became the first non-Big Five side to beat four Big Fives in a row since Johann Cruyff and Ajax Amsterdam in 1971-72.</p><p>England, France, Germany, Italy and Spain comprise the Big Five.</p><p>Kudos to Knutsen, too, for reportedly spurning advances from other squads, including Celtic.</p><p>To me, the CL is the planet’s premier sporting event. The cream from Europe’s top-flight divisions qualify for the following season’s CL, which can be a nine-month trek. In late summer, 82 squads from 53 associations began the battle.</p><p>Mix that in with a player’s domestic regular-season and tournament (sometimes two, as in England) responsibilities and national-side duties, and rest is rare for elite figures.</p><p>For example, Man City’s star Norwegian striker, Erling Haaland, 25, has logged 51 matches this season, with eight more coming in the Premier League plus World Cup fixtures.</p><h3>Whiffed on PSG</h3><p>With plenty of work and handicapping projects, I got distracted from last season’s CL. Unfortunate, since how I diagnose the Big Five would have led to investing in PSG, and profit.</p><p>The stellar resource FBRef produces Big Five pages, where I highlight GA90 (goals plus assists over 90 minutes) and opponents’ GA90 figures for respective offensive and defensive aggression barometers.</p><p>I would have had PSG circled, since its GA90 (4.65) rated second to Bayern Munich’s (4.76).</p><p>At some middle stage, a decent PSG futures ducat would have been obtained. In the quarters, with an eye on the future, PSG received a break by getting placed on the other side of the bracket from Munich.</p><p>That would have resulted in more positions with PSG, which eliminated Aston Villa. And Inter Milan dumped Munich, extra critical since the finale would be staged in Munich, to ease PSG’s path.</p><p>PSG beat Arsenal in their two-leg semifinal, where Inter eliminated Barcelona.</p><p>The Parisians then slammed Inter 5-0 for its first Champions League crown.</p><h3>Kane is able</h3><p>With Europe’s most lethal spearhead of Harry Kane, Luis Diaz and Michael Olise, Bayern Munich currently tops the Big Five GA90 with a sterling 6.11. Then comes Barcelona (4.62), PSG (3.85) and Inter (3.70).</p><p>(I prize key team figures to eliminate bias and opinion. Since FBRef started compiling stats in 1995-96, this Bayern side is the only one with a GA90 rating in the sixes.)</p><p>In differential, mixing in defensive GA90, it’s Munich (3.92), PSG (2.85), Inter (2.64) and Barça (2.63).</p><p>This time around, PSG’s Bayern-avoidance luck evaporated as it plays Liverpool on the same side of the bracket as Real Madrid-Bayern Munich.</p><p>The other side is Barcelona-Atletico Madrid and Sporting Lisbon-Arsenal.</p><p>I grabbed a 12-1 title ticket on Munich in September.</p><p>Die Roten has outscored foes 39-12 in its current 10-0-2 stretch, so Monday I invested more, at +325.</p><p>I also got a +750 title position on Real Madrid.</p><h3>Bianco bits</h3><p>This season’s May 30 title venue is Budapest, and Carmine Bianco favors PSG to -repeat, giving it a semifinal edge over -Munich.</p><p>WagerTalk’s ace Toronto-based soccer ’capper, Bianco believes pressure is affecting Arsenal boss Mikel Arteta.</p><p>“Winning the English Premier League is Arteta’s goal,” Bianco said, “more so than the CL, as weird as that sounds.”</p><p>CL champion squads earn €25 million, runners-up €18.5 million.</p><p>Bianco already had a +700 PSG title ticket, and he nabbed +550 last weekend. He said Atletico, 20-1 at DraftKings, has underdog value.</p><p>“They can defend,” he said, “and really don’t fear any side.”</p><p><b>CHAMPIONS LEAGUE </b></p><p><b>Title odds</b></p><p><b>Odds Team</b></p><p>+225 Arsenal</p><p>+340 Bayern Munich</p><p>+500 Barcelona</p><p>+600 PSG</p><p>+900 Real Madrid</p><p>12-1 Liverpool</p><p>18-1 Atlético Madrid</p><p>65-1 Sporting Lisbon</p><p>Source: Circa Sports<br></p>

entries.title: SAVE Act looks like racist laws of the past
entries.summary: <p>When Kansas implemented its version of the Trump administration’s looming <a class="Link" href="https://campaignlegal.org/document/fact-sheet-save-act-threatens-all-voters" target="_blank" >voter-suppression law</a>, it denied at least <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/24/us/politics/proof-of-citizenship-voter-registration-kansas.html?unlocked_article_code=1.WFA.M6Rq.JGLPfJQF4PSr&smid=url-share" target="_blank" >31,000 eligible citizens</a> of their constitutional right to vote.</p><p>On the national level, it could block <a class="Link" href="https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/analysis-opinion/new-save-act-bills-would-still-block-millions-americans-voting" target="_blank" >tens of millions</a> of Americans from casting a ballot.</p><p>That is, of course, exactly what the so-called Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act, or SAVE Act<i>, </i>is intended to do.</p><p>Donald Trump himself is on record admitting his belief that allowing all eligible citizens to vote would <a class="Link" href="https://wapo.st/4rYIqMT" target="_blank" >hurt his party’s chances</a> of winning elections. Republican Sen. Mike Lee of Utah <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DV84MHDFMuL/" target="_blank" >posted polling data </a>showing that his party is expected to lose control of the Senate this year and claimed the voter suppression bill could “turn this around.” </p><p>The president told his political allies earlier this month that his voter-suppression bill would <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/homenews/house/5776058-trump-republicans-save-america-act-midterms/#:~:text=Trump%20tells%20Republicans%20the%20SAVE,create%20new%20barriers%20to%20voting." target="_blank" >“guarantee” their success</a> not only in this year’s midterm elections but “every election for a long time.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c70000" name="module-c70000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>In fact, he is so desperate to keep American citizens from voting that he <a class="Link" href="https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2026/03/23/homeland-security-tsa-shutdown-trump-airports/89285094007/" target="_blank" >blew up a deal</a> to end chaos at the nation’s airports, holding Transportation Security Administration funding hostage unless the voter suppression bill is passed.</p><h3>Millions would be sidelined</h3><p>The bill that passed the House last month and is currently before the Senate would prevent <a class="Link" href="https://www.brennancenter.org/our-work/analysis-opinion/anti-voter-save-act-must-be-stopped" target="_blank" >more eligible citizens from registering to vote</a> than any piece of legislation in American history and is more restrictive than the current law in any state except Ohio. Far from a simple “voter ID” bill, it would require specific proof of citizenship to register to vote. </p><p>For most Americans, that means either a U.S. passport or a certified birth certificate along with a government-issued photo ID. But more than 21 million Americans don’t have ready access to those documents, and about half of Americans don’t have a passport at all. Not all birth certificates<a class="Link" href="https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/senate-bill/1383/text#H704CC9D148E248EBB87A96421701221B" target="_blank" > meet the requirements</a> — about 69 million women and 4 million men do not have a birth certificate that matches their current legal name.</p><p>Already registered to vote and think the voter suppression law won’t apply to you? You could be in for a nasty surprise. The bill requires voter roll purges<i> every 30 days</i>, eliminating the 90-day protection from being mistakenly thrown off the rolls right before Election Day. Voters might not realize they’ve been purged until they show up at their polling place — and most of us will have to show up at a polling place. Even though eight states and the District of Columbia conduct <a class="Link" href="https://bipartisanpolicy.org/report/mail-voting-is-safe-secure/" target="_blank" >safe and secure</a> elections entirely by mail, the bill prohibits universal <a class="Link" href="https://wapo.st/4rXx1gl" target="_blank" >mail voting</a>.</p><p>Anti-democracy activists have been invoking the <a class="Link" href="https://www.brennancenter.org/topics/voting-elections/vote-suppression/myth-voter-fraud" target="_blank" >myth of voter fraud</a> to justify voter suppression for <a class="Link" href="https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2019/01/the-200-year-history-of-using-voter-fraud-fears-to-block-access-to-the-ballot/" target="_blank" >nearly two centuries</a>, but have never been able to back up their claims with evidence. In 1883, Chicago party officials <a class="Link" href="https://www.motherjones.com/politics/2019/01/the-200-year-history-of-using-voter-fraud-fears-to-block-access-to-the-ballot/" target="_blank" >hired investigators</a> to monitor polling locations and offered a $300 reward to anyone who helped “in the apprehension and conviction of anyone who voted illegally.” No one was convicted.</p><p>An official who testified in defending the Kansas law admitted that over a span of 18 years, only 13 non-citizens had ended up on the voter rolls in Sedgwick County, none of which could be shown to be intentional rather than administrative errors. Only five noncitizens actually cast a ballot.</p><p>The secretary of state who unsuccessfully defended the Kansas voter suppression law, Kris Kobach, also <a class="Link" href="https://www.usnews.com/news/national-news/articles/2018-01-10/trump-commision-on-election-integrity-found-no-evidence-of-voter-fraud" target="_blank" >failed to unearth evidence of voter fraud</a> as vice-chair of the “Presidential Advisory Commission on Election Integrity” established in 2017 to appease Trump’s embarrassment over losing the popular vote.</p><p>False claims of fraud were integral to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/GPO-J6-REPORT/html-submitted/ch3.html" target="_blank" >fake elector plot</a> that culminated in the Jan. 6 insurrection, but none of the <a class="Link" href="https://electioncases.osu.edu/case-tracker/?sortby=filing_date_desc&keywords=&status=all&state=all&topic=25" target="_blank" >more than 60 lawsuits</a> challenging the results of the election produced <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/homenews/campaign/3559758-conservative-group-finds-absolutely-no-evidence-of-widespread-fraud-in-2020-election/" target="_blank" >any credible evidence of fraud</a>, much less non-citizen voting.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2024/10/12/nx-s1-5147789/voting-election-2024-noncitizen-fact-check-trump" target="_blank" >rhetoric</a> that the Trump administration and its allies are using to promote the specter of noncitizen voting echoes the white nationalist <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/what-is-great-replacement-theory-and-how-does-it-fuel-racist-violence" target="_blank" >“Great Replacement</a>” conspiracy theory of a plot to diminish the influence of white people. </p><p>In 2024, then-Washington Post columnist Philip Bump called the voter suppression scheme the <a class="Link" href="https://www.washingtonpost.com/politics/2024/07/09/save-act-republican-voting-immigration/" target="_blank" >Great Replacement theory in legislation form</a>. The legislation itself, which imposes <a class="Link" href="https://racism.org/articles/citizenship-rights/voting-rights/12869-the-save-america-act" target="_blank" >onerous financial burdens</a> on voters, is nothing short of a resurrection of the poll taxes of the Jim Crow era. And just like the racist Plessy v. Ferguson decision that gave rise to that dark period, the SAVE America Act belongs on the scrap heap of history.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/marc-h-morial" target="_blank" ><i>Marc H. Morial</i></a><i> is president and CEO of the National Urban League</i> <i>and</i> <i>was mayor of New Orleans from 1994 to 2002.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-a80000" name="module-a80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>

entries.title: Dear Abby: Friend with benefits was never really even a friend
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> Six months ago, I started spending time with a woman I knew casually for about a year following her traumatic breakup with her ex-boyfriend. At first, we were just friends, but it quickly progressed as we opened up and shared everything about ourselves with each other. She continued insisting we were "just friends" and went out with other men, which, at the time, didn't bother me.</p><p>Two months in, we started being intimate. Three months ago, she began referring to me, her puppy and herself as "a family," talking about future kids and grandkids. She asked to meet my parents and made sure I met hers when they came to town. I began believing this was going to be a long-term thing, even though she still insisted we were just friends.</p><p>Three weeks ago, she met and started dating another guy. She told me she still wants to be best friends, that she had been "50-50" the entire time about whether to date me but couldn't commit because she wasn't physically attracted to me, despite having sex with me for many months. She said she was physically attracted to me sometimes but didn't feel that way all the time — and she thought she should be. I cut things off with her. I'm extremely hurt, and I don't understand her decision. Can you help? — <i>REJECTED IN COLORADO</i></p><p><b>DEAR REJECTED:</b> I can try. The "friend" you were dating and intimate with may be an adult chronologically, but she is emotionally immature. Intense physical attraction is great while it lasts, but in many cases, it diminishes with time. Lasting relationships like marriage depend upon more than that to succeed. Give her marks for honesty and be glad you found out what her true nature is, but also realize that you dodged a bullet. All women are not like her, and you will meet one you can trust.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> A friend of mine recently got married. We are pretty close. I was asked to be a bridesmaid. (Unfortunately, I had to decline due to scheduling conflicts.) I attended the wedding, bringing an appropriate gift, only to find out a few months later that the marriage was never legally registered. It had just been a commitment ceremony. When I asked why she didn't announce it as such, she said she wanted people to think it was a real wedding.</p><p>I'm feeling a bit betrayed, and I wonder how her other guests would feel if they found out. I'm not sure I would have bought such an expensive gift if I had known beforehand. Is there a rule of etiquette for this sort of thing, or am I overthinking it? — <i>WONDERING IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR WONDERING:</b> Your reaction is understandable. While no formal rule of etiquette forbids lying to one's friends and loved ones, the Bible has something to say about it. Your friend wanted a party. She wanted gifts. She and her boyfriend did not want a legal commitment that lasts a lifetime or they would have had an actual wedding.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Abby shares more than 100 of her favorite recipes in two booklets: "Abby's Favorite Recipes" and "More Favorite Recipes by Dear Abby." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $16 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Cookbooklet Set, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>

entries.title: How we photographed ICE agents working at O'Hare Airport
entries.summary: <p>This week’s top photo comes from Visual Journalist <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/authors/anthony-vazquez" target="_blank" >Anthony Vazquez</a>. Follow him on <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/avazquezphoto?utm_source=ig_web_button_share_sheet&igsh=ZDNlZDc0MzIxNw==" target="_blank" >Instagram</a>.</p><h3>What makes this photo important?</h3><p>This image captures the recent usage of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents in the role of TSA officers. Seeing ICE and TSA personnel side-by-side is a visual representation of how the transition has taken effect. Many lawmakers have pushed back on the use of ICE agents in airports — as have travelers — due to the agency's history of racial profiling and the wrongful detention of U.S. citizens. For many, their presence represents a move toward more aggressive policing in public spaces, turning a routine travel gate into a source of fear and anxiety.</p><h3><b>How did you get the photo?</b></h3><p>This photograph was captured during a trip to O'Hare International Airport in Chicago, following news reports of ICE agents being deployed to airports due to TSA shortages. Initially, the deployment to O'Hare was unconfirmed, leading to a "goose chase" between different terminals to verify the reports. The situation changed as ICE officers made their presence more visible. Their masks potentially contributed to their reduced concealment, making documentation easier. This particular image was obtained at a Terminal 1 exit where the agents were openly present, standing guard alongside TSA workers.</p><h3>Technical details:</h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" entries.title: Inspired by Artemis II? Adler Planetarium can give you the moon
entries.summary: <p>In 1969, humanity achieved one of its greatest milestones when astronauts first set foot on the moon. More than half a century later, we’re <a class="Link" href="https://www.floridatoday.com/story/tech/science/space/2026/03/24/artemis-ii-moon-mission-targets-april-1-launch-nasa-working-no-issues-kennedy-space-center/89298116007/?gnt-cfr=1&gca-cat=p&gca-uir=true&gca-epti=z113734p001350c001350v113734d--37--b--37--&gca-ft=63&gca-ds=sophi" target="_blank" >preparing to return</a>.</p><p>NASA’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/4/3/23668863/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis" >Artemis</a> program, which followed the successful uncrewed Artemis I mission in 2022, is advancing towards Artemis II, the first crewed deep space lunar flyby of this new era. Soon, astronauts will once again travel beyond Earth’s orbit and around the moon.</p><p>This is an extraordinary moment in space exploration. But it is also a reminder of something deeply human: our enduring fascination with the moon and our desire to understand our place in the universe.</p><p>More than 50 years after Apollo, there is still much left to uncover. Humans returning to the moon is not about nostalgia. It is about discovery. It is about science. Studying the moon can help us better understand Earth, the solar system and what it will take for humans to venture further into space.</p><p>Chicago has a special role to play in this moment.</p><p>Space missions may launch far from Chicago, but the sense of discovery they inspire belongs to all of us. Institutions like the <a class="Link" href="https://www.adlerplanetarium.org/" target="_blank" >Adler Planetarium</a>, the oldest planetarium in the western hemisphere, connect the public to that story, translating complex space science into experiences that spark curiosity and understanding.</p><p>At a time when science is often misunderstood or dismissed, places like the Adler help people understand how scientific discovery works. Visitors can look through telescopes, experience immersive dome shows and meet the astronomers and educators working to interpret new discoveries about our cosmic neighborhood. When guests ask questions about Artemis or about what comes next in space exploration, they can hear directly from experts who study the universe every day.</p><p>That connection matters.</p><p>Space has a unique ability to inspire curiosity across generations. When a child asks why the Moon changes from night to night, or when a family gathers around a telescope to observe the sky together, they are participating in the same sense of discovery and connection that has driven human exploration since millennia.</p><p>As Artemis prepares to carry humanity back to the Moon, Chicagoans have an opportunity to reconnect with that exploration — and a front-row seat to the discoveries ahead.</p><p><i>Elizabeth C. Babcock, president and CEO, Adler Planetarium</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-2a0000" name="module-2a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Pass on gas </h3><p>Should we allow ourselves to be led around by the nose by daily reports of the ups and downs of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/economy/2026/03/04/chicagoans-uneasy-gas-prices-iran-war-straight-hormuz-supply-chain-disruptions" >prices at the pump</a>, as though that is the one best gauge for the success or failure of the party in power?</p><p>What a trap — prices are up so we can gloat that a man most of us detest and the party that has elevated this monster for its own gain are failing in their promise to lower the cost of living. What happens when the price goes down? You want to laud Republicans then?</p><p>What hypocrisy for progressives (as I count myself) to shout ourselves hoarse about the real dangers of our warming planet, then complain about gasoline costing $4, or $5, or even $6 a gallon. To achieve the pressing goal of weaning ourselves from fossil fuels, gas should be $20 a gallon! Don't scoff at this.</p><p>The Republicans and their monster salesman have removed our great nation from the international climate agreement, rolled back federal initiatives to phase out coal and accepted hundreds of millions of dollars in bribes from oil producers to put their extraction back on the fast track while tearing down popular programs to promote renewable energy.</p><p>The sooner we, as citizens of a powerful and influential nation, join much of the rest of the world in grasping control of fossil fuel use, the better off the whole world will be.</p><p>If oil and natural gas were taxed to increase their price, intelligent Americans would drive less, stop sitting in idling cars, learn to use public transportation (which would then become better), use less heating and air conditioning and use fewer disposable goods.</p><p>If we put one-tenth of our military budget into achieving the goal of drawing down fossil fuel use, the future would look brighter for our children.</p><p>It starts in our everyday life, and that includes the gas pump. Don't get suckered into the short-sighted narrative of gas prices.</p><p><i>Kenneth Stein, Lombard</i></p><h3>Loyalty replaces thought</h3><p>George Orwell’s deepest warning in "1984" was not simply that those in power lie. Governments have always lied. His more unsettling point was that power can train people to accept each new lie as truth, even when it contradicts the lie that came before it. The goal is not merely deception. It is submission.</p><p>That is what made Ingsoc, the book's antagonists, so disturbing. The party did not just demand obedience in action — it demanded obedience of mind. It wanted people to accept each new version of events without insisting on consistency, memory or proof. The point was not honest persuasion. The point was to sever loyalty to reality and attach it instead to authority.</p><p>That part of Orwell’s vision feels painfully current. We live in a moment when many people no longer seem troubled by contradiction. An explanation is offered for some act of power, and days later the explanation changes. Yet those most committed absorb the new line and move on as if nothing happened.</p><p>This is not ordinary persuasion. It is the replacement of judgment by allegiance. What matters is not whether the explanation is true, but whether it came from the approved authority. That is why exposing a lie has become so difficult. When belief is rooted not in evidence but in devotion, facts lose much of their force. It was never accepted because it was proven — it was accepted because it came from the right source and served the right side.</p><p>I say that without innocence. I have seen good in political traditions that later disappointed me. The temptation to surrender judgment exists across political life. Belonging becomes dangerous when it asks us to stop thinking. That is why the problem is moral before it is partisan.</p><p>The real danger is not simply that falsehood spreads, but the habit of resisting falsehood disappears. Once people stop demanding consistency, proof and seriousness from those who seek power, they begin helping power erase reality itself.</p><p><i>Jodi Alain Weinstein, Naperville</i> </p><h3>Baffled by city’s hotel tax hike</h3><p>Am I the only Chicagoan confused after reading reporter Fran Spielman's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/18/hotel-tax-increase-tourism-improvement-district-choose-chicago" target="_blank" >story</a> last week headlined "To pay for tourism boost, Chicago ramps up hotel tax, making it nation's highest"?</p><p>The city of Chicago, prompted by the marketing group Choose Chicago, seeks to increase tourism by raising the city’s hotel tax to 19% — the highest in the nation. Simple logic and basic arithmetic together confirm that raising prices, and reducing any competitive advantages, reduces sales or tourist attraction to Chicago hotel rooms in 14 wards. This does not make sense.</p><p>Do they seek to make Chicago "the first city," rather than "the second city" in the wrong direction?</p><p>Last year, Choose Chicago adopted a new marketing slogan, "Never Done. Never Outdone." They unknowingly evoked arch-rival New York City, "the city that never sleeps."</p><p>I am confused. Can anyone help me?</p><p><i>Harvey J. Graff, Lincoln Park</i></p><h3>Mayor was right to veto wage hike freeze </h3><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson did the right thing by <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/25/brandon-johnson-veto-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants" >vetoing the bill</a> that would freeze the minimum wage for tipped workers. Everyone knows tipping is customary, but it’s certainly not mandatory. A tip, whether it materializes or not, and its size, are completely at the whim of customers. Workers' livelihoods shouldn't be contingent on the whim of customers. And restaurateurs must figure out a way not to run their businesses on the backs of their hardest-working employees.</p><p><i>Joanne G. Murphy, West Ridge</i> </p><h3>Basking in strangers’ warmth while dining alone </h3><p>Just recently, a long-time friend and I made plans to meet at a restaurant for lunch. Unfortunately, my friend never showed up. She was OK and just needed to reschedule.</p><p>Early bird that I am, I had waited for some time for my friend in a comfortable enclosed area where diners entered and exited at a steady pace. It was the perfect place for observing people of all ages and backgrounds.</p><p>Three uniformed police officers were just leaving after enjoying their tasty breakfast. They held the door for four older adults, who all patted the officers on their forearms and told them, "God keep you safe" and "thank you for your service." The officers told the seniors that they truly appreciate their prayers and thoughts.</p><p>Eating alone, I witnessed many more acts of kindness, from a waiter carefully wiping up a rambunctious child's spill to a young woman helping her grandmother butter her toast. I heard the phrases, "Thank you," "God bless you," "have a wonderful day," "let's keep in touch," "so wonderful to see you" and "let's do this again sometime."</p><p>Did I feel left out? Not a bit, because I knew my friend and I would meet another day. Americans spend quite a bit of money at restaurants and maybe we sometimes regret that. But I dare say it is worth it, especially if we go back home satisfied, both physically and spiritually.</p><p><i>Kathleen Melia, Niles</i></p><h3>Unfortunate word choice</h3><p>I feel that it was extremely irresponsible journalism for the Sun-Times to echo the Donald Trump/Republican racist and jingoistic verbiage as it did with Monday’s large print screaming headline, "Venezuelan Migrant Charged With Murder Of Loyola Student." Neither the nationality, ethnicity or immigration status of the man who allgedly perpetrated this <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/ice-detainer-loyola-student-killing-sheridan-gorman" >horrible murder</a> is of any relevance, except to those for whom these categories determine the significance of events long before they occur. </p><p>In short, any crazed person might have done this, and deep down, most of us adults know this. Those at the Sun-Times taxed with creating the headlines, and even more so, those higher up in the newsroom who do the editing and revising, should take a long look at themselves in the mirror.</p><p><i>Steve Livingston, North Park</i></p><h3>Anti-immigrant rhetoric harmful and divisive </h3><h3></h3><p>Any time anyone in Chicago picks up and fires a gun at another person, it is a tragedy. As we mourn the death of Sheridan Gorman and try to make sense of a seemingly senseless act of violence, MAGA politicians from <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration" >Donald Trump</a> to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/24/chicago-crime-trump-pritzker-bovino-immigration-midway-blitz-deport-immigration" >Darren Bailey</a> to 45th Ward <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/reel/DWR7pCAjX7d/?igsh=bzY5eXJleHRxdGFo" target="_blank" >Ald. Jim Gardiner</a> are politicizing her death to score points against their rivals and demonize immigrants. It’s the same tune they’ve sung for years: stoke fear against the most powerless groups to keep us divided and distracted. Shame on these men, and my condolences to the Gorman family, who must now grieve privately while politicians turn their sadness into a public spectacle.</p><p><i>S. Gronkiewicz-Doran, Jefferson Park</i></p><h3>Paydays for ICE — not TSA </h3><p>I fail to comprehend why U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement personnel receive <a class="Link" href="https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/why-do-ice-agents-get-paid-during-the-partial-government-shutdown-but-not-tsa" target="_blank" >government compensation</a> while Transportation Security Administration employees do not during the partial government shutdown.</p><p><i>Diane Blaszczyk, Norwood Park</i></p><h3>Expensing Trump’s trips </h3><p>Prior to the last election, Donald Trump wanted very much to get into the White House. Now that he is the president, instead of staying in the White House, he makes <a class="Link" href="https://www.palmbeachpost.com/story/news/2025/05/03/trump-100-days-news-president-mar-a-lago-palm-beach-florida/83404838007/?gnt-cfr=1&gca-cat=p&gca-uir=false&gca-epti=z110301p000450c000450e007150v110301&gca-ft=35&gca-ds=sophi" target="_blank" >numerous trips</a> to Mar-a-Lago at the <a class="Link" href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2025/mar/09/trump-golf-trips" target="_blank" >nation’s expense</a>. It's not only the cost of the flight but also the cost for the <a class="Link" href="https://gisme.georgetown.edu/publications/secret-service-costs-for-presidential-travel-continue-to-mount/" target="_blank" >Secret Service</a>, his entourage and other officials. Considering the nation's financial status, Trump should be made to reimburse the Treasury Department for these expenses. America cannot afford this extravagance.</p><p><i>Larry E. Nazimek, Logan Square</i></p><h3>Robotic moves </h3><p>The delivery robots that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/25/delivery-robot-smashes-into-old-town-bus-shelter-in-second-crash-of-the-week" >shattered the glass</a> at CTA bus shelters have been the object of misplaced derision. They deserve our praise for locating the most pristine, graffiti-free pane of glass in all of Chicago's CTA bus shelters. Perhaps a historical marker in their honor?</p><p><i>Judy Humphrys, Roscoe Village </i></p>

entries.title: Macquline King, CPS grad and principal who led through mass school closures, rises to CEO
entries.summary: <p>Macquline King was a young principal working hard at a small South Side elementary when she got word that her school was slated to close.</p><p>Teacher Claire Brandon remembers watching King fight to keep Dumas Technology Academy open “to the bitter end” alongside parents and staff.</p><p>“It was a hurtful time,” she says, remembering the many protests and hearings where King and parents begged school district officials for a reprieve, to no avail.</p><p>Not long after she hugged the students at Dumas goodbye, King was thrust into another tense situation. She became principal of Courtenay Language Arts Center on the North Side, which was designated to take in children grieving their own closed school.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Parents Felicia Suggs and Yolanda Harris spoke out against the closing of Dumas Elementary in March 2013. Dumas staff remember how King fought alongside them and parents to try and keep the school open.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>John H. White/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>King says it was “horrible” watching one community lose its school and then difficult to bring together two schools that had different needs and demographics. At Courtenay, she says, she “began to really understand how policy played a large role in what happened in the day-to-day activities at a school.”</p><p>Those experiences propelled her to seek out a broader leadership position. It's also why some see her as uniquely qualified to lead the district as it <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/09/25/chicago-public-schools-enrollment-drops-restarts-decrease-migrant-students-latino-immigration" target="_blank" ><u>grapples with a big drop in enrollment</u></a> and current and future budget deficits.</p><p>She says the experiences taught her the importance of listening and not trying to tackle problems alone.</p><p>“I really believe in shared leadership,” she says. “Chicago is so big and it's so diverse, and so going around having round tables, having learning sessions, that's really important to me. Because I can't bring all of the families and the students into the building, but I can go out into the community to elevate their voices.”</p><p>The Chicago Teachers Union Vice President Jackson Potter says students need King to “meet the moment.” And he agrees she can’t do that solo.</p><p>“It will require her to lead a team in partnership with the mayor, board members, and educators who have been pushing to reconstruct and transform the district,” Potter said in a statement.</p><h3><b>King built leadership skills as Chicago principal</b></h3><p>After a nationwide search, it’s surprising in some ways that the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/26/cps-board-selects-macquline-king-as-permanent-ceo-will-vote-on-her-contract-monday" target="_blank" ><u>school board chose King</u></a>.</p><p>She originally <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/11/05/cps-ceo-search-macquline-king" target="_blank" ><u>wasn’t named a finalist for the permanent job</u></a>. Now the board is scheduled to vote to approve King as CEO on Monday, and will offer her a three-year contract.</p><p>Before becoming interim CEO 10 months ago, she’d never run a school district. She has not even run a Chicago Public Schools department or headed a network, which would have put her in charge of around 20 schools.</p><p>After spending 15 years as a principal of the two small elementary schools, she went to work on policy in former Mayor Lori Lightfoot's office and was kept on by Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>As interim CEO, King greeted students on the first day of school in August 2025 at Courtenay Elementary, the school she led as principal as it took in students from a nearby school that closed.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>King also doesn’t have a lot of experience lobbying for state policy or working with lawmakers at a time when CPS is about to have a fully elected board and is in desperate need of more state funding.</p><p>And she hasn’t created a budget for a big complex system like CPS. She and her staff are in the process of developing the spending plan for next school year.</p><p>However, the school board made <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/03/20/cps-school-board-votes-to-require-next-leader-be-an-educator-not-a-ceo" target="_blank" ><u>having a superintendent’s license a new requirement</u></a> for the CEO position, signaling that they wanted someone more steeped in teaching and learning than finance or bureaucracy.</p><p>In other ways, her background makes her ideal for the job. Teachers, parents and a former top Johnson aide all lauded King.</p><p>Tonda Lee, whose son transferred into Courtenay elementary from a school that closed, says she remembers King as a “lovely lady.” King won over several parents who carried their anger about their school closing into Courtenay.</p><p>“She's one that actually thinks about the parents and families. She has a way of bringing people together,” she says.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>King spent 15 years as a principal in CPS, including at Courtenay Elementary in Uptown. She developed a reputation for being a caring leader who wanted to help staff improve.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Lillian Mongeau Hughes/The Hechinger Report</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Brad Worrell, who taught under King for eight years, said he often left meetings held by King feeling “motivated and empowered” and that she had “tremendous” leadership skills.</p><p>“She is not afraid to roll up her sleeves and get into the dirty work,” said Worrell, who now teaches in Indiana. “She pushed us.”</p><p>Worrell says King also has a playful side. He still has a video of King competing against Courtenay students in a sack race, noting how she put her all into trying to get in front of the kids.</p><p>“She was in it to win it,” Worrell said.</p><p>Brandon, the Dumas elementary teacher, followed King to Courtenay. She says King sees “treasures” in people that they might not see in themselves. And, King knows how to provide criticism to her staff and then support them to improve, Brandon says.</p><p>Brandon says it is <a class="Link" href="https://hechingerreport.org/hire-black-principals/" target="_blank" ><u>rare as a Black woman to find a boss that looks like her</u></a> and is “caring, loving, supportive, and knows her stuff.”</p><h3><b>King’s time as CPS student and parent give her unique lens</b></h3><p>Like her two most recent predecessors, King is a homegrown talent.</p><p>She grew up on the South Side and in a twist that’s emblematic of CPS’ history, the schools she attended as a child and teen — Oakenwald North Elementary and a <a class="Link" href="http://www.metrohschicago.com/book.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>high school called Metropolitan School of Studies</u></a> — are now closed.</p><p>She earned a scholarship to attend Olive Harvey College, a City College of Chicago, and then went to the University of Illinois Chicago where she intended to become a pharmacist. But she says she fell in love with teaching while volunteering and changed course. She got her teaching and graduate degrees from National Louis University.</p><p>King is the mother of two grown sons who also attended and graduated from CPS. She says raising children in the city gave her another lens for seeing the system. She remembers how difficult it was to navigate the high school application process, and then her boys ended up on opposite ends of the city — one at Von Steuben in North Park on the North Side and the other at Kenwood on the South Side.</p><p>Getting to report card pickup at both schools from her teaching job was a struggle. “I had that battle,” she says.</p><h3><b>Lessons learned during time as interim CEO</b></h3><p>As interim CEO, King took some bold stances. But in other situations her response was tentative. Some criticize that hesitancy, while others say she was up against some unprecedented situations at a time when she didn’t have a secure leadership position.</p><p>Last June, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/06/26/interim-chicago-public-schools-ceo-pegs-deficit-at-730-million" target="_blank" ><u>she inherited a budget deficit</u></a> originally pegged at $734 million, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/08/14/majority-of-cps-board-demands-a-budget-with-pension-payment-and-loan-rebuffing-proposal-from-cps-leaders" target="_blank" ><u>got into a public disagreement</u></a> with the mayor’s office and school board allies over borrowing and then spent the fall <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/12/02/as-immigration-agents-swept-chicago-this-fall-communities-stepped-in-to-get-kids-to-school-safely" target="_blank" ><u>trying to protect students and families</u></a> from President Donald Trump’s aggressive immigration enforcement operation.</p><p>More recently, she and her staff have had to figure out how to address a charter school network that ran out of money, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/19/former-aspira-students-try-to-adapt-after-troubled-charter-operator-closes-schools-midyear" target="_blank" ><u>forcing the unheard-of midyear displacement of nearly 600 high schoolers</u></a>.</p><p>Depending on where people sit, King gets different reviews.</p><p>She surprised many when she stood up to the mayor and his allies on the school board by refusing to draft a budget that could have put the district further into debt. The mayor’s people not only took issue with her decision, but they also said she didn’t communicate well with them. Initially, they did not see her as a contender for the permanent post.</p><p>Yet others applauded the stance, noting that CPS is already deep in debt and doesn’t need to take on more.</p><div data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>As interim CEO, King got praise and pushback for putting forward a budget that did not include the possibility of taking on more debt.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Her ability to stand up to that kind of pressure and really decide what was in the best interest, I believe, for CPS speaks volumes about her integrity, and so in that regard, I appreciate her leadership,” said 40th Ward Ald. Andre Vasquez, who represents parts of Edgewater and Lincoln Square on the North Side and is the chair of the Latino caucus.</p><p>That independence impresses Vasquez so much that he supports her, but he still wants to see Latinos in positions of power in CPS, where 46% of students are Latino. <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2026/03/18/chicago-public-schools-lacks-latino-ceo-finalists/" target="_blank" ><u>Some Latino leaders</u></a> criticized the school board for <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/13/cps-board-names-3-finalists-to-lead-district-interim-ceo-king-former-nyc-east-baton-rouge-schools-chiefs" target="_blank" ><u>not having any Latinos in the most recent finalist pool</u></a>.</p><p>Vasquez points to the school district’s fragmented response to the aggressive immigration enforcement this fall as evidence that CPS needs leadership who “understands the particular challenges faced by the Latino community and the undocumented Latino community.”</p><p>It took King and her team a month and a half to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/12/no-recess-lockdowns-less-learning-how-immigration-agents-disrupted-cps-schools-this-fall" target="_blank" ><u>adjust protocols around when to lock down schools if federal agents were nearby</u></a> and to <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/09/cps-ice-immigrant-student-families" target="_blank" ><u>create a 24-hour command center</u></a> that schools could go to for help.</p><p>King also insisted that the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/23/cps-school-board-remote-learning-immigration-ice-deportation-raids" target="_blank" ><u>state needed to give CPS permission</u></a> to put a remote learning option in place and, at least publicly, did not push Gov. JB Pritzker to allow it.</p><p>Vasquez says that a Latino leader might have pushed harder for virtual learning for families that were afraid to bring their children to school.</p><p>“I think those are pressures that if perhaps you've not experienced it or come from a community that's experienced it, you wouldn't have the appreciation for the dire need,” Vasquez said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/01/29/ctu-wants-to-bargain-with-cps-over-remote-learning-options" target="_blank" ><u>The Chicago Teachers Union has asked</u></a> King to bargain over issues related to protecting students and families should there be another surge of federal immigration agents coming into Chicago. So far, she has refused.</p><p>Still, Potter says he’s impressed with how King has kept her cool under pressure. CPS needs that as Trump continues to attack public education, immigrants, LGBTQ people and programs that support poor families, he says.</p><p>“We need someone who's going to go to bat for kids, and for school funding and take on the racist administration that is constantly attacking our city and our schools,” he said.</p><h3><b>Issues ahead: declining enrollment and troubled charter schools</b></h3><p>King typically reads prepared statements at board meetings, but at the October meeting her voice shook as she tried to address <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/23/cps-school-board-remote-learning-immigration-ice-deportation-raids" target="_blank" ><u>members’ concerns about the district’s response to immigration enforcement</u></a>.</p><p>She said she and her team were fielding calls at all hours about “every single detainee,” as well as other serious issues like gun violence.</p><p>“We have to carry that every day,” she said, pleading with board members to tap into their networks to help the school district.</p><p>As she <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/10/23/cps-school-board-remote-learning-immigration-ice-deportation-raids" target="_blank" ><u>gets ready to take the permanent position</u></a>, King says one of the biggest challenges is trying to keep students safe. In order to do that, she says the district has to work collaboratively with the city and other agencies.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-9b0000" name="image-9b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
