srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6841d86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Fdd%2Fda0d84424efb9ed94053eec9dc92%2Fcpsduallanguage-101725-12.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b3609d8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Fdd%2Fda0d84424efb9ed94053eec9dc92%2Fcpsduallanguage-101725-12.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6841d86/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5998x3366+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2Fdd%2Fda0d84424efb9ed94053eec9dc92%2Fcpsduallanguage-101725-12.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>In October 2025, King greeted students at Calmeca Academy of Fine Arts in Brighton Park as they studied math in Spanish.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times file</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“It was humbling to understand just the power that a safe and trusted environment can have in the face of something so catastrophic as the federal activity,” she says. It reinforced “that this leadership role is not one that you can do on your own.”</p><p>Andrew Broy, president of the Illinois Network of Charter Schools, said King also has been in a tough position as she’s had to deal with privately run public schools that are in trouble financially. That includes the Aspira charter school network, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/19/cps-revokes-aspiras-ability-to-operate-charter-schools-in-chicago-in-rare-move-board-of-education" target="_blank" ><u>which ran out of money and will officially close next week</u></a>.</p><p>Some board members are diametrically opposed to both charters and the idea of shutting down schools. That has resulted in “haphazard” decisions, he says.</p><p>When asked about charter schools, King doesn’t say whether or not she generally supports them. She says they are part of the CPS system and that she sees this moment as one where their role within the district “can be clarified.”</p><p>King also doesn’t rule out closing district-run schools.</p><p>There’s a growing buzz among fiscal conservatives and even some school board members and candidates that this needs to happen. <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/chicagos-50-closed-schools" target="_blank" ><u>Since CPS closed 50 schools in 2013</u></a>, only a few additional district-run campuses have been shuttered. But CPS has about 85,000 fewer students than it did in 2014 and 44 district-run schools have dwindled to fewer than 200 students.</p><p>King, however, bristled at the idea that schools should be closed to save money.</p><p>“I do not want to reduce our students to dollars and numbers,” she said.</p><p>She says her time at Dumas and Courtenay taught her <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2018/05/22/study-2013-chicago-school-closings-failed-to-help-students" target="_blank" ><u>how much students and communities are impacted when their schools close</u></a>.</p><p>The focus, King says, must be on student experiences at really small schools that don’t have the budgets or students to host a variety of classes and clubs.</p><p>“Does the building itself or the programming or the funds set the conditions for us to fulfill our mission?” she says. “Are those students receiving the same experience as a student that’s in a school with a larger enrollment?”</p><p>While King acknowledges CPS is facing many complex issues, she says she’s looking forward to trying to set up the school system for the future. Artificial intelligence and other new technologies are reshaping the job market and she’s worried that if leaders don’t prepare students, they could be left behind.</p><p>“The landscape of education, the way we teach, hasn't really changed that much,” she says. “But society and the jobs that we're preparing students for are changing quite a bit.”</p>
entries.author: Sarah Karp | WBEZ

entries.title: Despite limited ice time, Sam Lafferty is beloved by the Blackhawks: 'He's just a joy to be around'
entries.summary: <p>NEW YORK — Every morning at the recently renamed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> Ice Center, Connor Bedard and Sam Lafferty take over the team ping-pong table for a series against each other.</p><p>And every time, Lafferty wins.</p><p>"I haven't beaten him yet this year," Bedard said Friday. "Every time it's Game Six or Game Seven, he takes me down."</p><p>They're similarly inseparable at the card table on team flights or at the dinner table at restaurants on the road, except they let others join them for those activities.</p><p>They've played a few rounds of golf, too, and Bedard cherishes the one time in Los Angeles he got to partner with Lafferty instead of having to compete against his supposedly incredible golfing skills.</p><p>They've become extremely close friends, with Bedard estimating he has possibly spent more time with Lafferty this season than anyone else on the team. The 30-goal scorer's appreciation for the one-goal scorer is immense.</p><p>"I don't know how many games he's played this year, but he's the most positive guy at the rink every single day," Bedard said. "There hasn't been one day this year where he's been down or sulking. He comes in and is cracking jokes every day. He's just a joy to be around."</p><p>On the ice, Lafferty's second stint with the Hawks has not been nearly as impactful as his first (in 2021-22 and 2022-23), and it's nearing an end with nine games left in the season.</p><p>The now-31-year-old forward has, as referenced, just one goal — actually a highlight-reel beauty Nov. 12 against the Devils — and one assist in 26 games. He has been a healthy scratch a whopping 47 times and has averaged only 8:30 of ice time when in the lineup.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">WHAT A GOAL FROM SAM LAFFERTY THAT WAS DISGUSTING 😱🤢 <a href="https://t.co/Af2EHznvvk">pic.twitter.com/Af2EHznvvk</a></p>— Gino Hard (@GinoHard_) <a href="https://twitter.com/GinoHard_/status/1988835877821284387?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">November 13, 2025</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>His willingness to maintain such a good attitude about that minuscule role has been valuable for the Hawks in numerous ways, though.</p><p>For one thing, it provides roster flexibility to have a guy on hand who can step in when needed — due to injuries or illness — and won't protest if weeks go by without injuries or illnesses. That allows the Hawks to not have to disrupt prospects' development by shuffling them back and forth between Chicago and Rockford.</p><p>Lafferty's jovial, lighthearted personality has also boosted the mood in the locker room, in the gym, on the bus and everywhere else. He's a positive influence off the ice.</p><p>"I just try to go about my business every day and take the mindset, 'What can I do? What can I control to try to help the team?'" Lafferty said recently.</p><p>"I love this group of guys. I think the sky is the limit, and we just want to keep pushing every day. It's cool to see some of these young guys growing and taking their futures to the next level. ... Even though I haven't played as much as I would like, it's been a blast coming to the rink every day with this group."</p><p>Ask any Hawk about Lafferty and a smile instantly spreads across their face.</p><p>"He's one of the best guys, honestly," Nick Lardis said. "He's always in such a great mood, whether he's in or out of the lineup. He's just nice to all these guys, and he's honestly a good guy to learn from, too."</p><p>"He's a great teammate, first and foremost — a guy you can talk to, joke around with," Ryan Donato said. "[Even though] I'm sure he'd like to play more, you really couldn't tell. He doesn't let it faze him. He doesn't let himself or his teammates get dragged down by it. ... He never lets us know for a second that he's upset. He's always working extremely hard."</p><p>Lafferty may have no bigger fan than Bedard, though.</p><p>"[He's] one of my favorite people I've met in hockey," Bedard said. "I'm grateful to have played with him this year."</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Man fatally shot inside Englewood home
entries.summary: <p>A man was shot and killed in his Englewood home early Saturday, police said.</p><p>The man, 45, was in his home in the 5700 block of South May Street around 5 a.m. when someone fired shots from inside a passing car, according to Chicago police. The car fled the scene.</p><p>The man was shot in the head and was pronounced dead at the scene.</p><p>No one is in custody and detectives are investigating.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: 'No Kings' rallies kick off in Evanston, Cicero and Lincoln Square as hundreds expected to protest in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>As Joanne Bertalan sat in her wheelchair on a crisp and sunny morning during Evanston's "No Kings" rally, she thought back to the civil rights protests she attended as a college student at Bradley University in 1964. </p><p>“We’ve been here before,” said Bertalan, 77. “These people have all lived through a lot of stuff and wars. It’s got a lot of veterans here and they're done with this.”</p><p>She was among a few hundred people gathered Saturday in Evanston to kick off protests taking place across the country. The nationwide rallies, dubbed "<a class="Link" href="https://apnews.com/article/no-kings-protests-minnesota-trump-springsteen-2fab6b3a64e5275bcf111e8dd6d2e075" target="_blank" >No Kings</a>," are protesting President Donald Trump's policies including immigration, the rollback of transgender rights and the war in Iran. </p><p>Organizers expect more than 3,100 events to take place in the U.S., and protests also were planned for Europe, the AP reported. Rallies are planned Saturday for Grant Park, Lincoln Square and Irving Park in Chicago. Just outside of the city, other rallies will take place in Cicero, Oak Park, Forest Park and Evergreen Park.</p><p>Last fall, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/live/2025/10/17/no-kings-protests-chicago-suburbs-trump" target="_blank" >tens of thousands of people attended similar protests</a> across the Chicago region. Republicans had branded the protests as the "Hate America Rally."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e90000" name="image-e90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c81f6de/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b7af7d6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71948ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Cheree Bertalan, left, sits next to her mother, Joanne Bertalan, during Saturday's "No Kings" rally in Dawes Park in Evanston. The mother and daughter walked with their church to join the protest. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71948ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/025e9f5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/71948ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F11%2F2f7feef54feebbf72293e5c355a0%2Fimg-7890.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cheree Bertalan (left) sits next to her mother, Joanne Bertalan, during Saturday’s “No Kings” rally in Dawes Park in Evanston. The mother and daughter walked with their church to join the protest. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Leigh Giangreco/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In Evanston, the crowd skewed grayer, with demonstrators carrying not only posters but canes, walkers, beach chairs and wheelchairs. Bertalan attended the rally with her 57-year-old daughter, Cheree Bertalan. Both attended the 'No Kings' rally last year and returned for the event, noting they appreciated the peaceful demonstration that brought out dogs, grandmothers and young children alike.</p><p>Evanston Mayor Daniel Biss, who won the Democratic primary for the Illinois’ 9th District Congressional seat, took the stage around 10:45 a.m. to loud cheers.</p><p>“Things have gotten worse,” Biss said. “ There is a war, an unjust, illegal, deranged war. We endured a violent federal occupation in our streets, here in Evanston and across the Chicago area throughout the fall, and then they went on to Minneapolis, where they just started shooting people.“</p><h3>Fighting for rights ‘no matter what’ in Cicero</h3><p>Outside of Cicero Town Hall, a crowd of 60 people gathered, holding signs stating, "Ice Out," "No Kings" and "Stop the War." </p><p>“No hate, no fear, immigrants are welcome here!” they shouted at one point, as cars on West Cermak Road honked in approval.</p><p>The protest was organized by the Cicero Berwyn Community Web to galvanize the predominantly Latino community. Volunteers said they wanted to provide a welcoming, accessible and joyful way for people to protest, especially for those who can't make it to Grant Park. They provided snacks, chairs and decorations, including piñatas designed to resemble Trump and Cicero Town President Larry Dominick.</p><p>Volunteer Melissa Mouritsen, who held an upside-down U.S. flag, said she was protesting authoritarianism and reflecting on nation’s forthcoming 250th anniversary.</p><p>“They want us to be scared,” she said. “They want us to think their ICE agents at the airport are going to make us afraid to travel or live our lives, and we’re not going to do that. We’re the inheritors of the Declaration of Independence. We’re the inheritors of the rights and liberties, and we are going to fight for them no matter what. But we’re going to do it joyously.”</p><p>A U.S. Marines veteran, who identified himself as Jose Manuel, spoke out at the rally about the amount of money Trump has spent on the war in Iran and the conflict between Israel and Hamas in Gaza territory within Palestine. </p><p>“When we ask for investments here at home for schools, housing, and mental health care, we’re told there isn’t enough,” said the 38-year-old. “But there is always enough money for war.”</p><p>Stickney resident Rae Disco, 46, and her two daughters, Lilith, 7, and Elly, 13, were among those in the crowd. </p><p>“The easiest possible thing anybody can do to support this cause is to just make your voice heard,” Disco said. “So I’m training my two daughters not to blindly accept any government or authority. I want them to be autonomous, and part of that means that we have to fight for what is, constitutionally, our rights.”</p><p>Disco said her primary concern was the rollback of reproductive rights, as well as Trump’s mass deportation campaign. Her daughter Elly said she felt like she was living in a dystopian novel.</p><p>“It’s something that future generations are going to look back on and can’t believe is happening,” Elly said. </p><h3>Children join Lincoln Square rally</h3><p>Hours before thousands of people were expected to march downtown, a smaller but impassioned group gathered in Lincoln Square.</p><p>Around 100 people, including children, gathered in Welles Park carrying signs opposing the president. People brought their kids and dogs, and used to chalk to write messages like "Choose love” and “Love thy neighbor."</p><p>The group sang, "This is for our people who are locked inside, together we will abolish ICE," before marching the perimeter of the park.</p><p>Brenna O’Brien, a local parent who organized the protest as an alternative to the Grant Park rally, addressed the group before the march.</p><p>“We’re not loyal to a mad king trying to take over the country,” she said to cheers. “Chicagoans take care of each other and we will not let him take over this city.”</p><p>Meagan Moore, of Lincoln Square, took her 6-year-old son, Theo, and 4-year-old daughter, Ada, to the protest. The children made their own signs, Moore said.</p><p>“This is a picture of people fighting and I put an X over it,” Theo said, holding his sign that read, “No more wars.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-dd0000" name="image-dd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6c7b3b1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F35%2Ff7cfcbc44fb9848086fc7b28d28b%2Fimg-1819.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a650eab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F35%2Ff7cfcbc44fb9848086fc7b28d28b%2Fimg-1819.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/279d9a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F35%2Ff7cfcbc44fb9848086fc7b28d28b%2Fimg-1819.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Meagan Moore, of Lincoln Square, took her two children, Ada and Theo, to Saturday's smaller protest in Chicago's Lincoln Square neighborhood. She said it was important to show her children that their opinions matter. “They see what’s going on. [I want to] let them know that they can use their voice and say when things aren’t going how they’re supposed to," she said. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/279d9a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F35%2Ff7cfcbc44fb9848086fc7b28d28b%2Fimg-1819.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/867e989/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F35%2Ff7cfcbc44fb9848086fc7b28d28b%2Fimg-1819.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/279d9a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4032x2263+0+381/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F35%2Ff7cfcbc44fb9848086fc7b28d28b%2Fimg-1819.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Meagan Moore, of Lincoln Square, took her two children, Ada and Theo, to Saturday’s smaller protest in Chicago’s Lincoln Square neighborhood. She said it was important to show her children that their opinions matter. “They see what’s going on. [I want to] let them know that they can use their voice and say when things aren’t going how they’re supposed to,” she said. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mary Norkol/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Selena G., who declined to give her last name, brought her 6-year-old son to the Lincoln Square protest. Her parents weren’t politically involved, she said, and she wasn’t very informed until she was older. She wants to change that with her son. </p><p>Selena said the intense immigration activity last year rocked her Albany Park community, and that was one of many reasons why she showed up to Saturday's protest. She's gotten involved in her community by helping walk other children to school and passing out whistles in public places so neighbors could alert one another about ICE activity. </p><p>That has given her solace. </p><p>“Sometimes it just feels good to know that everyone else is also upset,” she said. “I find that this is really uplifting even though everything else is kind of terrible.”</p><p><i>Check back for updates.</i> </p>

entries.title: Teen shot and killed in Brighton Park
entries.summary: <p>A teenage boy was fatally shot in the Brighton Park neighborhood early Saturday, police said.</p><p>The boy, 17, was walking outside in the 2400 block of West 48th Street around 1 a.m. when a red sedan drove past him and someone inside fired shots, according to Chicago police. </p><p>The boy was taken to Mount Sinai Hospital with multiple gunshot wounds to his body. He was initially listed in critical condition and later pronounced dead.</p><p>No one is in custody and detectives are investigating.<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Prospects shine at Triple-A after White Sox' Opening Day dud
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — As bad as Opening Day was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/26/white-sox-opening-day-loss-brewers-shane-smith-chase-meidroth" target="_blank" ><u>for the Sox</u></a>, Friday night’s season opener for the Charlotte Knights was blooming with tantalizing possibilities for the franchise’s next wave of talent — including the two biggest arms that could point which way this rebuild is going.</p><p>In his first Triple-A start, left-handed 2024 first-round pick <b>Hagen Smith</b> gave up a run but struck out four over three innings of work.</p><p>Oswego East product <b>Noah Schultz</b>, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/28/sox-pitcher-noah-schultz-says-first-spring-start-was-one-to-build-on" target="_blank" ><u>towering 2022 first-rounder</u></a>, fanned five over four hitless frames in relief.</p><p>Shortstop prospect <b>William Bergolla Jr.</b> went 4-for-5 with two doubles, and following up his World Baseball Classic run <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/19/sam-antonacci-returns-to-spring-training-says-wbc-experience-was-definitely-worth-it" target="_blank" ><u>with Team Italy</u></a>, Springfield native <b>Sam Antonacci</b> smacked a three-run homer en route to the Knights’ 19-2 blowout over the Durham Bulls.</p><p>Antonacci, an infielder by trade, started in left field, a switch that could help grease the skids toward his big-league debut with the Sox.</p><p>“[He’s] going to play some outfield in Charlotte to start the year in an effort to get him comfortable with it," Sox player development director <b>Paul Janish</b> said. "There’s a chance that he gets asked to do that at the Major League level. If that’s the case, we want to have him prepared to do it if and when the time comes.”</p><h3><b>Coming and going</b></h3><p>Before their second game of the season, the Sox optioned veteran lefty reliever <b>Tyler Gilbert</b> to Charlotte and claimed southpaw <b>Bryan Hudson</b> off waivers from the Mets.</p><p>Additionally, catcher <b>Korey Lee</b> cleared waivers and reported to Triple-A for the Sox after winding up the odd man out to <b>Reese McGuire</b> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/22/white-sox-sign-catcher-reese-mcguire-leaving-korey-lee-as-odd-man-out" target="_blank" ><u>in spring training</u></a>.</p><p>Gilbert, notably <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/09/26/tyler-gilbert-glove-sticky-mlb-confiscate" target="_blank" ><u>absolved of sticky-glove mischief</u></a> last season after umpires confiscated his mitt during a game, was roughed up for two runs in an inning of work on Opening Day.</p><p>It’s the second Sox stint for Hudson, the 6-8 native of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/08/05/bryan-hudson-white-sox-mariners" target="_blank" ><u>Alton in southern Illinois</u></a>, who gave up three runs in four relief outings late in 2025. The journeyman’s best year came in 2024 with the Brewers, when he posted a 1.73 ERA over 43 outings.</p><h3><b>Mune-ball</b></h3><p>While <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> builds his fan base in Chicago, he’s making new ones in Milwaukee, too.</p><p>The Sox’ slugging rookie gave a signed bat to Brewers fan <b>Steve Pollack</b> in return for the ball Murakami launched into the right-field seats for his first career home run.</p><p>“This is awesome,” Pollack said outside the visitors’ clubhouse Thursday. “Just too cool.”</p><p>Murakami was sending the ball home overseas, where he swatted 246 dingers for the Yakult Swallows of Nippon Professional Baseball.</p><p>“It was a special occasion,“ Murakami said via translator <b>Kenzo Yagi</b>. “In Japan… the fans tend to return the ball back, so I’m relieved it came back to me.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-220000" entries.title: Cubs notebook: Dansby Swanson looking forward to future with Nico Hoerner
entries.summary: <p><b>Dansby Swanson </b>is one of the best defensive shortstops in baseball. Last season, Swanson was tied for third among National League shortstops with seven defensive runs saved and has won a pair of Gold Gloves.</p><p>Having <b>Nico Hoerner </b>a few feet to his left doesn’t hurt.</p><p>“Nico makes my life a lot easier, just his instincts, his presence, his ability to communicate,” Swanson said of Hoerner, a two-time Gold Glove second baseman. “Obviously, we’ve built that rapport with one another now for three years. It’s nice having that consistency and someone you can trust on the other side.”</p><p>That relationship will extend well past 2026 because of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/26/cubs-to-extend-second-baseman-nico-hoerner" >Hoerner’s six-year contract extension</a>, which is expected to become official soon. Swanson is signed through 2029, keeping the middle of the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a>’ defense intact for the rest of the decade.</p><p>Teammates since 2023, Swanson and Hoerner know each other very well. During their three-plus seasons together in the middle of the diamond they’ve built a connection that doesn’t arrive overnight and helps in every aspect of their games.</p><p>“It takes intentionality to build that relationship with one another and to understand strengths and weaknesses,” Swanson said, “and have an understanding of where he’s going to be, when he’s going to be at certain spots, what we need to communicate, what we can almost-telepathically communicate to one another.”</p><p>Telepathy aside, the extensions for Hoerner and center fielder <b>Pete Crow-Armstrong </b>plus the long-term contracts for Swanson and third baseman <b>Alex Bregman </b>give the Cubs a core of plus fielders at premium positions.</p><p>“We have some of the best in the game [on defense],” Swanson said. “To be able to make other teams earn their runs and their hits, dictates winning. The [front office has] been able to build out this roster in that fashion.”</p><h2><b>Appreciating Heyward</b></h2><p>Former Cubs outfielder <b>Jason Heyward </b>officially announced his retirement Friday, ending a 16-season career. Heyward compiled 41.5 wins above replacement (per Baseball Reference) and will be remembered by Cubs fans for his passionate rain-delay speech during Game 7 of the 2016 World Series.</p><p><b>Ian Happ</b> was not on that team, but Heyward still made a profound impact on Happ’s career as a teammate from 2017-22, influencing his professionalism, approach to the game and how he leads now. Happ said he texted Heyward on Friday, telling his former colleague how grateful he is to have played with Heyward for six seasons, thanking him for the “special memories” that were created during that time.</p><p>Happ also recalled how much time Heyward spent teaching him some finer points of playing the outfield. Considering the crush of outfielders vying for playing time when Happ broke in, Heyward could have chafed at tutoring a younger player.</p><p>Heyward did the opposite.</p><p>“We had a lot of guys playing outfield and jumping in and out; he had a ton of years at that time and a World Series ring and Gold Gloves, and to be OK with coming out, double-switching and things that were happening and to handle that and lift up the young guys, it’s just something that’s so impressive,” Happ said. “He always handled himself with grace and professionalism.”</p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow

entries.title: Cade Horton strong in 2026 debut, leads Cubs to 10-2 victory against Nationals
entries.summary: <p>Before the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" target="_blank" >Cubs</a>’ 10-2 victory Saturday against the Nationals, manager Craig Counsell said he could remember one or two starts in which right-hander Cade Horton struggled last season. Because of how nonlinear development is, Counsell predicted <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs/2026/03/17/cubs-pitcher-cade-horton-appears-to-be-back-on-track" target="_blank" >Horton</a> would have more bumpy games in 2026.</p><p>Dealing with occasional failures, Counsell said, might be a challenge Horton faces this season.</p><p>‘‘It’s adjusting to that and going from that,’’ Counsell said. ‘‘Cade handles that part really well. He turns it off and takes an objective look at it. He’s going to go through that, and you want to be ready to help him through that.’’</p><p>Horton’s first start of 2026 wasn’t one of those difficult outings. Horton, the runner-up in National League Rookie of the Year voting last season, allowed two runs and four hits in 6⅓ innings. He was efficient, too, needing only 75 pitches to get 19 outs.</p><p>Even with an eight-run lead, Counsell allowed Horton, 24, to begin the seventh — another sign that the Cubs expect him to lead the staff.</p><p>‘‘I prepare for it the same way I prepare for every year,’’ Horton said. ‘‘Just focusing on getting stronger and being able to manage my workload between outings has been the biggest thing for me. In terms of going deeper [in games], it really is about just getting built up and staying healthy.’’</p><p>Horton made 22 starts in 2025 and by the end of the season had emerged as the Cubs’ best starter. But his season was cut short by a rib fracture, ending his promising rookie year on a disappointing note and sidelining him for the playoffs.</p><p>‘‘It’s a new year; it’s exciting,’’ Horton said. ‘‘A lot of adrenaline, but it’s all about just controlling that and focusing on what I can control, and that’s executing pitches and living pitch to pitch.’’</p><p>The pitch that worked the best for Horton against the Nationals was his fastball, which he threw 45 times and for strikes 85% of the time. Though it’s classified as a four-seam fastball, the pitch moves and is difficult for opponents to pick up.</p><p>‘‘He’s throwing a 95, 97 mph fastball that’s cutting,’’ said left fielder Ian Happ, who hit a three-run home run during the Cubs’ four-run sixth. ‘‘I don’t think it’s the same shape back-to-back. Sometimes, it cuts and rises; other times, it dives down.’’</p><p>One reason Horton was able to pitch deep into the game was by not being reliant on strikeouts. He only got four and was content to let the Nationals make weak contact and set up the Cubs’ defense to make plays.</p><p>‘‘That power fastball that he has is so strong that it’s not easy for hitters to put in play,’’ said catcher Miguel Amaya, whose homer in the fourth was his first since May 19 of last season. ‘‘Whenever they make contact, it’s soft contact in the infield. And then attacking the zone is going to protect all the secondary pitches.’’</p><p>The Cubs hope the game was the start of Horton building off what he did last season. They have championship expectations, and Horton figures to be a key piece if they reach their goals.</p><p>‘‘He’s going to be a huge part of it,’’ Happ said. ‘‘He’s going to take the ball every fifth day. We need him. We need him to be [that] version of himself. It’s been impressive to watch. He’s going to continue getting better. That’s the fun part of having a guy like him on the staff.’’</p>
entries.author: Brian Sandalow
